tweets.text:
What just happened?

At 9:45 AM ET, the S&P 500 was trading nearly +1.5% higher and tech stocks were sharply higher.

At 10:40 AM ET, selling pressure began to intensify without any major headlines in one of the biggest reversals of the year.

Just 2 hours later, President Trump said Iran shot down a US helicopter "last night" and that the US must "respond to this attack."

This sent the S&P 500 to a new low of the day, down 240 points since 9:45 AM ET.

In just 3 hours, the S&P 500 has erased -$2.1 trillion in market cap.

Volatility is back.

tweets.text:
Rillet makes an ERP system that’s very different from competitors like NetSuite and Microsoft Dynamics: they “continually close” a ledger that’s always accurate, complete, and reviewable.

For companies using Rillet’s ERP system, the “Old Close” - meaning the bookkeeping entries that get manually reviewed and reconciled at the end of the period - is now just a tiny sliver of the ledger.

By and large, the books are being closed continuously, and that’s only recently become technologically possible.

Full breakdown: https://t.co/NUExKqdcPi

thread_tweets.text: @nicckopp And, sure enough, the type of entries you see on a ledger maps pretty reliably to what kind of data integrations are hooked up to the ERP: https://t.co/2oUhsugLJP
thread_tweets.full_text: @nicckopp And, sure enough, the type of entries you see on a ledger maps pretty reliably to what kind of data integrations are hooked up to the ERP: https://t.co/2oUhsugLJP

thread_tweets.text:
@nicckopp The “old-fashioned close” isn’t entirely gone, though; even with a modern setup like Rillet, there’s still some work going on at period-end.

Unsurprisingly, B2B companies have more than four times as much period-end work as B2C companies. https://t.co/U6gy1HuPFL
thread_tweets.full_text:
@nicckopp The “old-fashioned close” isn’t entirely gone, though; even with a modern setup like Rillet, there’s still some work going on at period-end.

Unsurprisingly, B2B companies have more than four times as much period-end work as B2C companies. https://t.co/U6gy1HuPFL

thread_tweets.text: @nicckopp So, just as B2B books have ~5x more period-end work, they also have ~5x more human-keyed entries, compared to consumer companies. https://t.co/dgitPmDIaZ
thread_tweets.full_text: @nicckopp So, just as B2B books have ~5x more period-end work, they also have ~5x more human-keyed entries, compared to consumer companies. https://t.co/dgitPmDIaZ

tweets.text:
BREAKING: Total onchain crypto card payment volumes hit a record $833 million in May 2026, up +180% year-over-year.

High performance networks such as Optimism and Solana have benefited from the growth in payment related activity.

These networks have outperformed the broader market since 2025 while capturing a combined ~22% of market share.

Key onchain players are accelerating adoption, such as Jupiter Spend through localized payment options such as QR integration, which has seen 60%+ month-over-month payment volume growth.

As a result, cumulative onchain crypto card payment volumes have exceeded $9 billion for the first time in history.

Onchain adoption is seeing rapid growth.
tweets.full_text:
BREAKING: Total onchain crypto card payment volumes hit a record $833 million in May 2026, up +180% year-over-year.

High performance networks such as Optimism and Solana have benefited from the growth in payment related activity.

These networks have outperformed the broader market since 2025 while capturing a combined ~22% of market share.

Key onchain players are accelerating adoption, such as Jupiter Spend through localized payment options such as QR integration, which has seen 60%+ month-over-month payment volume growth.

As a result, cumulative onchain crypto card payment volumes have exceeded $9 billion for the first time in history.

Onchain adoption is seeing rapid growth.

tweets.text:
The performance gap within tech stocks is at historic levels:

The top 20% of US tech stocks have outperformed the bottom 20% by ~120 percentage points over the last 3 months, the 2nd-highest reading in history.

By comparison, this difference peaked at ~135 percentage points during the 2000 Dot-Com Bubble.

This spread has QUADRUPLED over the last year, outpacing the surge seen in 1999-2000.

Top 20% of tech stocks have now returned +110% over the last 3 months, nearly matching February 2000 levels.

At the same time, the bottom 20% performers have returned -10%.

The tech rally has almost never been this concentrated.

thread_tweets.text: 'If you look at bitcoin vs other assets, from a valuation perspective it's cheap. If you wait till clarity act or some other analyst you may be too late, so taking advantage of selloffs like this makes sense.’ - Jim Ferrailoli, Schwab
thread_tweets.full_text: 'If you look at bitcoin vs other assets, from a valuation perspective it's cheap. If you wait till clarity act or some other analyst you may be too late, so taking advantage of selloffs like this makes sense.’ - Jim Ferrailoli, Schwab

thread_tweets.text: ‘There is a fundamental mismatch bt the growth of money in the economy and the amount of bitcoin available to buy. So what we look for when you want to be long crypto is when you expect an expansion of money and liquidity. The US debt is larger than the US economy and there’s historically one way govts get out of that situation: monetary inflation.’ - Jim Ferraioli of Schwab on what they look for as fundamantal case for bitcoin (which I totally i agree with, you HAVE to understand this to 'get' bitcoin imo, if not you'll see it al as pointless)
thread_tweets.full_text: ‘There is a fundamental mismatch bt the growth of money in the economy and the amount of bitcoin available to buy. tweets.full_text:
'This cycle bitcoin is a 40 vol asset, last one it was 60 and time before that it was 80' - Jim Ferraioli, Schwab's Dir of Crypto Research on btc getting more chill despite recent bear mkt, says it's suffering from strong momo elsewhere (AI, stocks). Also said 20% of the spot bitcoin ETF assets are via Schwab clients (I did not know that) w/ Ric Edelman on stage at DACFP.

'If you look at bitcoin vs other assets, from a valuation perspective it's cheap. If you wait till clarity act or some other analyst you may be too late, so taking advantage of selloffs like this makes sense.’ - Jim Ferrailoli, Schwab

tweets.text:
🇺🇸 What if the real #AI-driven #productivity cycle is only just beginning? 

📚 Major technological revolutions do not immediately translate into economic gains. With personal computers in the 1980s, and then the internet in the 1990s and 2000s, productivity gains came gradually, once companies had truly integrated these tools into their processes. At first, there is a lot of investment, experimentation, and sometimes even market excess. However, the real impact comes later, when the technology becomes a daily production tool.

➡️ This is probably where we are with AI today as the market has already priced in a large part of the first phase of the cycle. However, the real economy is only starting to understand how to use AI to produce more, faster, better, and with fewer frictions. The range of applications is huge and it goes far beyond technology companies alone. The real question for the coming years will therefore be whether companies can turn this innovation into measurable productivity gains. If AI helps reduce the time spent on repetitive tasks, improves decision-making, and accelerates production cycles, then it can become a genuine macroeconomic growth engine.

⚠️From an economic and organizational standpoint, the potential still appears far from fully exploited. The real turning point will come when AI is no longer just a market theme but a productivity reflex across the whole economy.

https://t.co/fbwzOKfmf8
tweets.full_text:

📚 Major technological revolutions do not immediately translate into economic gains. With personal computers in the 1980s, and then the internet in the 1990s and 2000s, productivity gains came gradually, once companies had truly integrated these tools into their processes. At first, there is a lot of investment, experimentation, and sometimes even market excess. However, the real impact comes later, when the technology becomes a daily production tool.

➡️ This is probably where we are with AI today as the market has already priced in a large part of the first phase of the cycle. However, the real economy is only starting to understand how to use AI to produce more, faster, better, and with fewer frictions. The range of applications is huge and it goes far beyond technology companies alone. The real question for the coming years will therefore be whether companies can turn this innovation into measurable productivity gains. If AI helps reduce the time spent on repetitive tasks, improves decision-making, and accelerates production cycles, then it can become a genuine macroeconomic growth engine.

⚠️From an economic and organizational standpoint, the potential still appears far from fully exploited. The real turning point will come when AI is no longer just a market theme but a productivity reflex across the whole economy.

tweets.text:
The 90D-SMA of XRP's Realized Profit to Loss Ratio has fallen to 0.38. For every dollar of loss being realized in the market, only 38 cents of profit is being taken.
At the 2025 peak, this ratio reached 50, meaning profit-takers were overwhelming loss-sellers by a factor of 50x. That dynamic has fully inverted.

A ratio this deep below 1 reflects a market where the majority of participants who are moving coins are doing so at a loss, a hallmark of intense capitulation.

📉 https://t.co/Yd5djeHMtc
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
The 90D-SMA of total fees paid on the XRP network has fallen from 5.9k XRP in Feb 2025 to 0.5k XRP today, a 91.5% decline.
A drop of this magnitude is not a fee market adjustment. It reflects a near-total contraction in organic transaction demand on the network since the https://t.co/yO5p6mWsCK
